Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Cross-Defendant Charles Engelber'S To Compel Additional Deposition Of Cross-Complainant Ellen Tsang And For Appointment Of Discovery Referee For The Deposition
Off Calendar Feb-16-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: Motion Of Cross-Defendant Charles Engelberg To Compel Additional Deposition Of Cross-Complainant Ellen Tsang And For Appointment Of Discovery Referee For The Deposition.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-Defendant Charles Engelberg's Motion To Compel Additional Deposition Of Cross-Complainant Ellen Tsang And For Appointment Of Discovery Referee For The Deposition is ruled on as follows. Cross-Defendant Charles Engelberg moves under CCP Section 639 and CRC, Rule 3.922. A discovery referee may be appointed on motion of any party where necessary "to hear and determine any and all discovery motions and disputes relevant to discovery in the action and to report findings and make a recommendation thereon." CCP Sec. 639(a)(5); CRC 3.920(a). Appointment of a discovery referee is authorized, however, only where "necessary" to hear and determine such motions or disputes. CCP Sec. 639(a)(5). An order appointing a referee may be upheld in "unusual" cases where a "majority" of the following factors exist: (1) multiple issues to be resolved; (2) multiple motions to be heard simultaneously; (3) present motion is only one in a "continuum" of many; (4) numerous and voluminous documents to be reviewed (especially in connection with issues based on assertion of a privilege) make the inquiry "inordinately time consuming." Taggares v. Sup.Ct. (Mitchell) (1998) 62 CA4th 94, 105-106-"Where one or more of the above factors unduly impact the court's time and/or limited resources, the court is clearly within its discretion to make an appropriate reference." While the JPT accessed the video of the deposition linked in D'Amico declaration and was able to view and listen to the first 20 minutes of the deposition (Repl., 4:13-14), no written transcript was provided in the moving papers. Therefore, it was difficult for the JPT to review the objections and instructions not to answer. To the extent that moving party takes issues with a specific objection made by opposing counsel, his remedy is file a motion to compel further responses (with a separate statement). Notwithstanding, a majority of the factors listed above are not present in this dispute regarding the deposition of Ellen Tsang. Therefore, the Motion is denied without prejudice.

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem at Peter.Catalanotti@wilsonelser.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Peter Catalanotti; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)

Off Calendar Mar-18-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Christopher M. Busch, Esq. of Coddington Hicks & Danforth, (650-592-5400), for Charles Engelberg, Cross-Defendant and moving party.
James P. Downs, Esq. of Law Offices of Wallace C. Doolittle, (510-888-0604), for Ellen Tsang, Cross-Complainant and opposing party.

ARGUED. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral arguments adopts the tentative ruling as follows: CROSS DEFENDANT CHARLES ENGELBERG'S Motion To Compel Cross-Complainant Ellen Tsang To Answer Deposition Questions And Produce Documents is
granted. (1) In deposition, witness is to respond to questions concerning identity of beneficiaries. The beneficiaries have only a limited privacy interest in their identities. Follow-up questions concerning beneficiaries will be limited to beneficiary contact information. Depending upon the age and relationship of the beneficiaries to the settlor(s), the personal knowledge of the beneficiaries (as opposed to hearsay information) is likely to be limited. (2) The trust agreement (with amendments) is to be produced at the deposition in redacted form to exclude all information except for (a) trustee appointment, designation, and acceptance, (b) authority or powers of the trustee, and (c) beneficiary identification. As to the authority and designation of the trustee, a review of the trust document is acceptable. The funding, distribution, and general operative terms of the trust, however, lie outside the scope of the litigation and are subject to the beneficiaries' and settlor's privacy privilege. Prevailing party to prepare order in compliance with CRC 3.1312(b). Judge Ronald Evans Quidachay, Clerk: M. Olopernes, Not Reported, D501.
